cards.title: Async/Await Pitfalls
takeaways: Never block on async (.Result/.Wait()) — go async all the way to avoid deadlocks and starvation.
Avoid async void (except event handlers); return Task so exceptions are observable.
Use ConfigureAwait(false) in libraries; don't fire-and-forget Tasks you care about.

cards.title: Cancellation & Timeouts
takeaways: Cancellation is cooperative — pass a CancellationToken through every async call; code checks it and throws OperationCanceledException.
A timeout is cancellation on a timer (CancellationTokenSource(TimeSpan)) — the key to not hanging on slow dependencies.
Link tokens to combine timeout + request cancellation; treat cancellation as expected, not an error.

cards.title: Channels & Producer/Consumer
takeaways: Channels give a thread-safe, async producer/consumer pipe — no manual locking.
A bounded channel provides backpressure: a full channel makes the producer wait, keeping memory bounded.
It's the modern async replacement for BlockingCollection and the building block for in-process pipelines.

cards.title: Concurrency vs Parallelism
takeaways: Concurrency = structure (tasks interleave, even on one core); parallelism = simultaneous execution (needs multiple cores).
Use async for I/O-bound work, parallelism for CPU-bound work.
They compose — a server is concurrent across requests and may be parallel within one.

cards.title: Locks & Race Conditions
takeaways: A race condition = correctness depends on thread timing; count++ is not atomic.
A lock gives mutual exclusion — keep the critical section tiny to limit contention.
Prefer Interlocked, concurrent collections, or immutability; acquire multiple locks in a consistent order to avoid deadlock.

cards.title: Thread Safety & Atomicity
takeaways: Prefer, in order: immutability → confinement → atomics (Interlocked) → concurrent collections → locks.
Thread safety needs both atomicity and visibility (a memory barrier), or threads won't see each other's writes.
The cheapest synchronization is none — immutable, unshared data can't race.

cards.title: Abstract Factory
takeaways: Abstract Factory creates families of related objects through one interface — guaranteeing they’re used together.
Swap the entire family by swapping the factory; the client (which uses only abstract products) doesn’t change.
Use it only when real product families exist — otherwise it’s heavier than a plain factory.

cards.title: Adapter Pattern
takeaways: Adapter converts an incompatible interface (adaptee) into the one your code expects (target).
It’s for integrating third-party/legacy code you can’t change — confine the foreign API behind one adapter.
Prefer the object adapter (composition + delegation) over the class adapter (inheritance).

cards.title: Boxing & Unboxing
takeaways: Boxing wraps a value type in a heap-allocated object (copy + allocation); unboxing extracts it back.
It's often invisible and cheap once, but boxing in hot paths causes allocations and GC pressure.
Generics (List<int>) avoid it — prefer generic APIs over object-based ones; watch struct-to-interface boxing.

cards.title: Builder Pattern
takeaways: Builder constructs a complex object step by step, replacing telescoping constructors with a readable fluent API.
Each step returns the builder; Build() validates invariants and returns the (often immutable) result.
For simple objects, C# object initializers / records with init often make a builder unnecessary.

cards.title: Chain of Responsibility
takeaways: A request travels a chain of handlers; each handles it or passes it on, decoupling sender from receiver.
Handlers can be added, removed, or reordered freely; any can short-circuit the chain.
The ASP.NET Core middleware pipeline is this pattern — the clearest real example.

cards.title: Command Pattern
takeaways: Command wraps a request (receiver + action + args) as an object with Execute() (and often Undo()).
Because the action is data, you can queue, log, schedule, and undo it — and the invoker is decoupled from the receiver.
It’s the basis of undo/redo, task queues, UI ICommand, and MediatR/CQRS commands.

cards.title: Composite Pattern
takeaways: Composite models part-whole trees; leaves and composites share one component interface.
The composite implements operations by recursing over children — clients treat the whole tree uniformly, with no type-checking.
File systems, UI/DOM trees, and org charts are classic composites.

cards.title: CQRS
takeaways: CQRS separates the write model (commands, state-changing) from the read model (queries, side-effect-free).
The light version is just separate handlers on one DB; the heavy version uses separate read/write stores synced by events (eventual consistency).
It’s not a default — apply it where read and write needs genuinely diverge; it adds real complexity.

cards.title: Decorator Pattern
takeaways: Decorator wraps an object in another with the same interface, adding behavior and delegating the rest.
Decorators stack — compose caching, logging, retry, etc. without a class per combination (composition over inheritance).
Same interface + adds behavior distinguishes it from Adapter (changes interface) and Proxy (controls access).

cards.title: Delegates & Events
takeaways: A delegate is a type-safe method reference (use Func/Action/Predicate); lambdas create them; they’re multicast.
An event is a restricted delegate: outsiders can only subscribe/unsubscribe (+=/-=); only the declaring class can raise it.
They make behavior a first-class value — the basis of callbacks, the observer pattern, and LINQ.

cards.title: Extension Methods
takeaways: An extension method is a static method (in a static class) with a this first parameter — called with instance syntax on types you don’t own.
It’s syntactic sugar: obj.Ext() compiles to Static.Ext(obj); no access to privates, no change to the type, visible only via using.
LINQ and ASP.NET Core’s fluent config are built on extension methods — the canonical examples.

cards.title: Facade Pattern
takeaways: A facade exposes one simple interface over a complex subsystem, reducing coupling to its internals.
It only simplifies — unlike a decorator (adds behavior) or adapter (changes the interface).
Any “service”/“manager” class that hides several lower-level steps is a facade; clients can still go deeper if needed.

cards.title: Factory Pattern
takeaways: A factory decouples object creation from use — callers depend on an interface, not new ConcreteType().
Simple Factory = one method picks the concrete type; Factory Method (GoF) = subclasses decide via a virtual creator.
It supports Open/Closed (add a type without editing callers); DI containers are the general-purpose factory.

cards.title: Generics
takeaways: Generics give type-safe, reusable code parameterized by type — no casting and no boxing for value types.
Constraints (where T : …) let you require a base/interface/new() so you can use T meaningfully.
The CLR specializes generics per value type but shares one implementation for reference types — fast and compact.

cards.title: LINQ Internals
takeaways: LINQ uses deferred execution — the query runs when enumerated, not when defined.
IEnumerable runs in memory via delegates/iterators; IQueryable builds expression trees a provider (EF) translates to SQL.
Don’t cross to IEnumerable too early (loses SQL translation), and avoid multiple enumeration (re-runs the query) — ToList() to materialize.

cards.title: Mediator Pattern
takeaways: Mediator routes interactions through a central hub so objects don’t reference each other — N×N coupling becomes N spokes.
The coordination logic lives in one place; keep the mediator focused so it doesn’t become a god object.
In .NET it’s MediatR: send a request → dispatched to a handler (plus notifications + a behavior pipeline) — the basis of CQRS.

cards.title: Observer Pattern
takeaways: Observer is a one-to-many dependency: the subject notifies all subscribers when it changes, without knowing who they are (loose coupling).
In C# it’s built in — delegate + event (+=/-=); IObservable<T> for value streams.
Unsubscribe to prevent the event-handler memory leak.

cards.title: Prototype Pattern
takeaways: Prototype creates objects by cloning a configured instance instead of constructing from scratch.
Mind shallow vs deep copy — shared nested references cause subtle bugs; deep-copy when you need independence.
Avoid ICloneable (ambiguous); prefer copy constructors or records’ with expression.

cards.title: Proxy Pattern
takeaways: A proxy shares the subject’s interface and controls access to it — virtual (lazy), protection, remote, or caching.
Same structure as a decorator, different intent: proxy controls access; decorator adds behavior.
EF Core lazy loading and gRPC clients are real-world proxies (virtual and remote).

cards.title: Records
takeaways: Records generate a concise, immutable data type with value-based equality, ToString, and deconstruction.
Equal by contents (not reference); 'change' via a with-expression that copies a new instance (non-destructive mutation).
Use for DTOs, value objects, events; record class (reference) vs record struct (value) — keep classes for identity-rich entities.

cards.title: Reflection & Attributes
takeaways: Reflection reads/uses type metadata at runtime — discover members, read attributes, create instances, invoke methods, with no compile-time reference.
Attributes are declarative metadata that only matter when something reflects over them; together they power DI, serializers, ORMs, validation, and test runners.
It’s slow and AOT/trim-hostile — cache results and prefer source generators for performance/AOT.

cards.title: Repository Pattern
takeaways: Repository gives a collection-like interface over data access, decoupling the domain from the DB/ORM and aiding testability.
EF Core’s DbContext/DbSet already are a repository + unit of work — wrapping them generically is often redundant and leaky.
When you do use it, prefer a repository per aggregate with intent-revealing methods over a generic Repository<T>.

cards.title: Singleton Pattern
takeaways: Singleton = private constructor + one static instance, exposed through a single access point.
Use Lazy<T> for correct lazy, thread-safe initialization — don’t hand-roll double-checked locking.
Prefer a DI container singleton (AddSingleton) over a global X.Instance — you get one instance and testability.

cards.title: Specification Pattern
takeaways: Specification encapsulates a business rule/query as a reusable object (IsSatisfiedBy + an expression).
Specs compose with And/Or/Not, eliminating duplicated Where predicates and keeping rules in one place.
Expose an Expression<Func<T,bool>> so EF translates it to SQL; pairs with Repository (Ardalis.Specification).

cards.title: State Pattern
takeaways: State turns each state into an object; the context delegates to its current state, which drives transitions.
It replaces sprawling switch (status) logic with a clean, extensible state machine (illegal actions rejected in one place).
Same structure as Strategy, different intent: states know each other and transition; strategies are independent.

cards.title: Strategy Pattern
takeaways: Strategy encapsulates interchangeable algorithms behind one interface and swaps them at runtime — composition over conditionals.
It replaces big switch/if ladders and supports Open/Closed (new algorithm = new class).
In C#, a Func<> delegate is often the whole strategy — see LINQ OrderBy and List.Sort.

cards.title: Template Method Pattern
takeaways: Template Method fixes an algorithm’s skeleton in a base method and defers variable steps to subclass hooks.
It’s the Hollywood Principle — the base calls your overrides; subclasses can’t change the structure, only the steps.
It’s the inheritance-based counterpart to Strategy (composition); prefer Strategy unless there’s a real is-a lifecycle.

cards.title: Unit of Work
takeaways: Unit of Work tracks all changes in an operation and commits them as one atomic transaction (all-or-nothing).
EF Core’s DbContext + SaveChanges are a Unit of Work — you use it constantly without naming it.
A custom IUnitOfWork helps share a transaction across repositories, but a thin wrapper over DbContext is often redundant.

cards.title: Value vs Reference Types
takeaways: Value types (structs) copy the whole value (independent); reference types (classes) copy the reference (shared object).
Value types are usually inline/stack (no GC); reference types live on the heap (GC-managed).
It drives default equality (contents vs identity) and mutation semantics — use structs for small, immutable, value-like data.

cards.title: Covariance & Contravariance
takeaways: Generics are invariant by default; variance allows safe substitution based on producer/consumer roles.
Covariance (out) = output/producer, preserves direction (IEnumerable<out T>); contravariance (in) = input/consumer, reverses it (Action<in T>).
Mnemonic: out = producer, in = consumer. Arrays are covariant but unsafely (runtime exception) — generics fixed that.

cards.title: yield & Iterators
takeaways: yield return produces a sequence lazily, one item at a time; the compiler generates an IEnumerator<T> state machine (same idea as async).
It enables deferred execution, memory-efficient streaming, and infinite/early-stop sequences.
LINQ operators are iterators — which is why LINQ is lazy and composable.

cards.title: Caching Strategies
takeaways: Cache-aside (app loads on miss) is the default; read-through moves that into the cache layer.
Write-through keeps the cache fresh (slower writes); write-back is fastest but can lose data.
Invalidation is the hard part — use TTLs + eviction on write, and guard against thundering herds.

cards.title: Connection Pooling
takeaways: Opening connections is expensive; the pool reuses open ones, keyed by the connection string.
Forgetting to dispose (or holding connections in long operations) causes pool exhaustion and timeouts.
Always using/dispose, keep work short, and size Max Pool Size to the DB’s limits.

cards.title: Deadlocks
takeaways: A deadlock is a circular lock wait; the DB detects it and aborts a victim transaction.
Prevent it with consistent lock ordering, short transactions, and good indexes (less locking).
They can’t be eliminated entirely — wrap write transactions in retry-on-deadlock logic.

cards.title: Reading Execution Plans
takeaways: The optimizer is cost-based and statistics-driven; the execution plan is the operator tree it chose.
Hunt for scans-instead-of-seeks, key lookups, and big estimated-vs-actual row gaps.
Stale statistics and parameter sniffing are the usual culprits behind a suddenly-slow query.

cards.title: Database Indexing
takeaways: An index is a sorted B-tree that turns a scan (O(n)) into a seek (O(log n)).
Clustered = the table’s physical order (one per table); non-clustered = a side structure pointing back to rows (many).
Indexes speed reads but tax writes and storage — and a function on the column disables them. Index deliberately.

cards.title: Isolation Levels
takeaways: Three anomalies — dirty read, non-repeatable read, phantom — are prevented by progressively stronger levels.
Read Uncommitted → Committed → Repeatable Read → Serializable: more consistency, less concurrency.
MVCC/snapshot isolation gives consistent reads without blocking writers; choose the lowest correct level.

cards.title: Normalization vs Denormalization
takeaways: Normalization (1NF–3NF) stores each fact once — write integrity, no update anomalies, but more joins.
Denormalization duplicates/pre-joins for read speed — faster reads, harder writes, risk of drift.
Normalize OLTP; denormalize read-heavy paths deliberately (read models, materialized views).

cards.title: NoSQL Patterns
takeaways: Four families: document (whole-entity), key-value (cache/sessions), wide-column (huge writes/feeds), graph (relationships).
NoSQL favors flexible schemas, horizontal scale, and denormalization — often BASE/eventual consistency over ACID.
Choose by access pattern; real systems mix stores (polyglot persistence).

cards.title: Redis Internals
takeaways: Redis is fast because it’s in-memory and single-threaded — no locks, and every command is atomic.
It’s a data-structure store (strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams), not just key→string.
Optional persistence (RDB/AOF) + TTL + eviction make it ideal for cache, sessions, rate limits, and leaderboards.

cards.title: Replication
takeaways: Leader–follower: writes to the leader, reads from followers — scales reads and provides HA standbys.
Async replication is fast but creates replication lag (eventual consistency); sync avoids loss but is slower.
Watch read-your-own-writes and failover; replication copies the whole dataset (vs sharding, which splits it).

cards.title: Partitioning & Sharding
takeaways: Sharding = horizontal partitioning across multiple servers, scaling writes and storage, not just reads.
The shard key is everything: even distribution (no hotspots) and alignment with your main query.
Cross-shard joins/transactions are hard — shard only after indexing, caching, and replicas fall short.

cards.title: SQL vs NoSQL
takeaways: SQL: structure, relationships, ACID, joins, strong consistency — choose for transactional/relational data.
NoSQL: flexible schema, horizontal scale, high throughput, BASE — choose by a known access pattern.
It’s usually both (polyglot persistence), with SQL guarding data that must be exact.

cards.title: Stored Procedures vs ORMs
takeaways: Stored procedures: reused plans, network-efficient, encapsulated — but logic in the DB, harder to test/version, DB-specific.
ORMs: productivity, type-safety, maintainability, migrations — but can emit inefficient SQL (N+1) and leak on complex queries.
Pragmatic answer: ORM by default, drop to raw SQL / Dapper / a stored proc for the hot, complex 10%.

cards.title: Transactions & ACID
takeaways: A transaction is an all-or-nothing unit: COMMIT applies everything, ROLLBACK undoes it.
ACID = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — the four guarantees relational DBs give you.
The write-ahead log delivers atomicity + durability: log to disk before applying, replay/undo on recovery.

cards.title: CI/CD Pipelines
takeaways: CI = integrate often + automated build/test on every push (fast feedback); CD = always-deployable, automated path to release.
A pipeline is a chain of gates producing an immutable artifact promoted across environments.
Small, frequent batches are safer than big releases — high deploy frequency and low failure rate go together.

cards.title: Containers & Images
takeaways: A container is an isolated process sharing the host kernel (light); an image is the immutable template it runs from.
Images are layered and cached — order the Dockerfile so dependencies precede source for fast rebuilds.
CI builds an immutable, versioned image in a registry; the exact tested artifact is what runs in prod.

cards.title: Deployment Strategies
takeaways: Rolling = gradual replace (cheap, no downtime); blue-green = two environments, instant flip + rollback (2× infra); canary = small slice first, ramp on health (smallest blast radius).
All require health checks and fast rollback; feature flags decouple deploying code from releasing it.
The trade-off is blast radius / rollback speed vs infrastructure cost and orchestration.

cards.title: Feature Flags
takeaways: A feature flag decouples deploy from release — ship code dark, expose it by config.
Enables gradual rollout (app-layer canary), trunk-based dev, A/B tests, and an instant kill switch.
Flags are debt — remove them after full rollout to avoid stale, untested branches.

cards.title: Infrastructure as Code
takeaways: IaC = infrastructure as version-controlled, declarative code — reproducible, reviewable, diffable.
Declarative + idempotent: describe desired state, apply repeatably with no duplicates.
Eliminates snowflakes and drift; enables identical environments and rebuild-from-code recovery.

cards.title: Native AOT
takeaways: Native AOT compiles to a self-contained native binary at build — no JIT, instant startup, low memory.
The cost: limited reflection, mandatory trimming, and platform-specific builds.
Use source generators to replace reflection; ideal for serverless, containers, and CLIs — not reflection-heavy apps.

cards.title: Assemblies & Metadata
takeaways: An assembly (.dll/.exe) = IL + metadata + manifest; it’s the unit of deployment, versioning, and internal access.
Metadata is self-describing, which is what makes reflection, serialization, and DI scanning possible — no header files needed.
The CLR loads assemblies into an AssemblyLoadContext (the modern AppDomain), enabling plugins and isolated unload.

cards.title: Async / Await Internals
takeaways: await frees the thread during I/O — it’s concurrency, not parallelism, and uses no thread while waiting.
The compiler rewrites each async method into a state machine; locals are hoisted so they survive the pause and resume.
Never .Result/.Wait() on async code — go async end-to-end and use ConfigureAwait(false) in libraries.

cards.title: Authentication & Authorization
takeaways: Authentication = who you are (builds a ClaimsPrincipal); authorization = what you may do (policies/roles/claims).
UseAuthentication must run before UseAuthorization in the pipeline.
401 = unknown/invalid identity; 403 = known but not permitted. Prefer policies over scattered role checks.

cards.title: Blazor Basics
takeaways: Blazor builds web UI from C# components (.razor) — markup plus an @code block, with binding and events.
Server runs UI on the server over SignalR (tiny download, needs a live connection); WebAssembly runs C# in the browser (offline-capable, bigger download).
.NET 8 unifies them with per-component render modes (Server / WebAssembly / Auto) on top of static SSR.

cards.title: The CLR
takeaways: C# → IL + metadata (compile time); the CLR JITs IL → native and runs it (runtime).
The CLR provides GC, type safety, exceptions, and threading — that’s what “managed code” buys you.
Modern .NET runs on CoreCLR, cross-platform; the old .NET Framework CLR was Windows-only.

cards.title: The Configuration System
takeaways: Config is layered providers merged into one IConfiguration; later sources (env vars, CLI) override earlier (JSON files).
The Options pattern binds a section to a typed class injected as IOptions<T> — no magic strings.
IOptions = startup snapshot; IOptionsSnapshot = per request; IOptionsMonitor = live reload.

cards.title: Dependency Injection
takeaways: DI hands a class its dependencies (via constructor) instead of it creating them — loose coupling and testability.
Three lifetimes: singleton (app), scoped (per request), transient (per resolve).
Never inject a shorter-lived service into a longer-lived one — that’s the captive-dependency bug.

cards.title: EF Core Internals
takeaways: The DbContext is a unit of work; the change tracker diffs entity states and SaveChanges emits batched SQL in a transaction.
LINQ over IQueryable becomes SQL on the server — don’t cross into IEnumerable too early.
Avoid N+1 with Include/projection, and use AsNoTracking for read-only queries.

cards.title: Garbage Collection
takeaways: The GC reclaims unreachable objects; it’s generational because most objects die young — gen 0 is cheap, gen 2 is costly.
The GC does not free unmanaged resources promptly — use IDisposable + using for those.
Reduce GC pressure by reducing allocations (pooling, Span<T>, structs), not by calling GC.Collect().

cards.title: Global Usings
takeaways: global using applies a namespace import to the entire project — declared once, not per file.
ImplicitUsings makes the SDK add the common namespaces for your project type automatically.
Keep them in one place (GlobalUsings.cs or the .csproj); they support static and alias forms too.

cards.title: gRPC
takeaways: gRPC is schema-first RPC: a .proto generates typed clients/servers; messages are binary Protocol Buffers over HTTP/2.
It’s faster and smaller than JSON/REST and supports four streaming modes — ideal for internal service-to-service traffic.
Browsers can’t call it directly (need gRPC-Web); keep REST for public, browser-facing APIs.

cards.title: Health Checks
takeaways: Health checks expose a standard verdict (Healthy/Degraded/Unhealthy) for orchestrators and load balancers.
Liveness failing → restart the pod; readiness failing → stop sending traffic (don’t restart). Keep them separate.
Register a check per dependency, tag them, and expose /health/live and /health/ready separately.

cards.title: HttpClientFactory
takeaways: new HttpClient() per request → socket exhaustion; one forever-singleton → stale DNS. The factory avoids both by pooling and rotating handlers.
Prefer typed clients (base address + calls in one class) injected via DI.
Attach Polly/standard resilience (retry, timeout, circuit breaker) at registration — transparent to callers.

cards.title: IHostedService
takeaways: IHostedService (StartAsync/StopAsync) runs background work tied to the host lifetime; BackgroundService wraps it in an ExecuteAsync loop.
On shutdown the host cancels the token — honor it to drain in-flight work gracefully.
It’s a singleton: create a DI scope per work item for scoped dependencies, and never let the loop throw out.

cards.title: JIT Compilation
takeaways: The JIT compiles IL → native per method on first call, then caches it.
Tiered compilation starts fast (Tier 0) and re-optimizes hot methods (Tier 1) in the background.
ReadyToRun pre-JITs at publish for faster cold start; Native AOT removes the JIT entirely.

cards.title: Logging
takeaways: Use message templates with named placeholders — structured properties are queryable; string interpolation is not.
ILogger is the abstraction; providers/sinks (Serilog, NLog) decide where logs go and can be swapped freely.
Pass the exception object to LogError(ex, …) and use scopes for correlation context.

cards.title: Memory Management
takeaways: Value types copy their data and are often stack-allocated; reference types live on the heap and copy a pointer.
Boxing turns a value type into a heap allocation — avoid it on hot paths (watch object/non-generic APIs).
Cut allocations with struct, Span<T>/stackalloc, and ArrayPool<T> to lighten the GC.

cards.title: The Middleware Pipeline
takeaways: A request flows through ordered middleware (the onion): inward to the endpoint, then back out through each layer.
Order matters — exception handling first, auth before authz, routing before endpoints.
Middleware that skips next() short-circuits the request (e.g. auth rejection, rate limiting).

cards.title: Minimal APIs
takeaways: Minimal APIs define endpoints as lambdas via MapGet/MapPost — no controllers, minimal boilerplate.
Parameters bind automatically from route, query, JSON body, and DI; return a value or an IResult for the status code.
Use MapGroup for shared prefixes/policies and endpoint filters for cross-cutting concerns; pick controllers when you want MVC’s richer conventions.

cards.title: Nullable Reference Types
takeaways: string = non-null, string? = maybe-null; the compiler warns on unsafe dereferences and tracks nullability through flow analysis.
It’s compile-time only — static analysis, not a runtime guard; nulls can still arrive from untyped sources.
The ! null-forgiving operator silences the compiler — use it sparingly; prefer real checks and required members.

cards.title: Pattern Matching
takeaways: Patterns test shape and bind data at once — type, property, relational/logical, positional, and list patterns.
The switch expression returns a value, turning branchy mapping logic into one readable block.
The compiler warns on non-exhaustive switches — lean on it, and use _ for the default arm.

cards.title: Records
takeaways: Records give compiler-generated value equality plus ToString/Deconstruct/copy from a concise declaration.
They’re built for immutable data: init-only props and non-destructive with copies.
Use records for DTOs/value objects/events; use class for entities with identity and behavior.

cards.title: SignalR
takeaways: SignalR gives real-time, bidirectional push via Hubs (RPC both ways) over a persistent connection.
It negotiates the best transport (WebSockets → SSE → long polling) automatically.
Scaling to multiple servers requires a backplane (Redis) so messages reach clients on every instance.

cards.title: Source Generators
takeaways: Source generators inspect your code at compile time and emit additional C# compiled with it — purely additive.
They replace runtime reflection (JSON, regex, logging), giving faster startup, fewer allocations, and AOT/trim safety.
Incremental generators cache their work so they don’t bog down the IDE.

cards.title: Span<T> & Memory<T>
takeaways: Span<T> is a zero-copy, bounds-checked window over array/stack/unmanaged memory — slicing allocates nothing.
It’s a ref struct (stack-only): no fields, closures, or use across await.
For async or stored scenarios use Memory<T> and get its .Span at the point of use.

cards.title: Task vs Thread
takeaways: Thread = a heavy OS resource you manage; Task = a lightweight future you compose with async/await.
Task.Run borrows a pool thread for CPU work; awaited I/O uses no thread while waiting.
Default to Task; use a raw Thread only for dedicated long-running work needing special control.

cards.title: The Thread Pool
takeaways: The pool reuses a small set of worker threads for short work items — Task.Run, continuations, timers, I/O callbacks all use it.
It grows slowly under load, so blocking pool threads causes thread-pool starvation (latency cliff).
The cure is async-all-the-way, not raising min threads.

cards.title: Arrays vs Linked Lists
takeaways: Array: O(1) index, O(n) middle insert/delete, contiguous + cache-friendly; linked list: O(1) insert/delete given the node, but O(n) to find it.
In practice arrays usually win — prefetching/cache locality beats pointer-chasing, and nodes add memory overhead.
Default to a dynamic array (List<T>); use linked lists only for genuine frequent O(1) end/known-node edits (e.g. LRU).

cards.title: Big-O Cheat Sheet
takeaways: Hash table = O(1) average, O(n) worst; balanced BST = O(log n); array = O(1) index but O(n) search.
Comparison sorts bottom out at O(n log n) — quicksort fastest in practice, mergesort stable but O(n) space, heapsort in-place.
Stable sorts preserve equal-element order; counting/radix beat O(n log n) only for bounded integer keys.

cards.title: Big-O Notation
takeaways: Big-O measures how cost grows with input size (worst-case, dropping constants/lower terms) — growth, not wall-clock time.
Know the ladder: O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2ⁿ) < O(n!).
The gap is huge at scale (an accidental O(n²) is the classic prod slowdown) — but consider constants at small n, and analyze space too.

cards.title: Hash Tables
takeaways: A hash table maps keys to buckets via a hash function → O(1) average insert/lookup/delete.
Collisions (chaining or open addressing) and a high load factor degrade it; resizing keeps buckets short — worst case is O(n).
Keys need consistent Equals + GetHashCode and should be immutable (mutating a key loses it).

cards.title: Sorting Algorithms
takeaways: Comparison sorts can't beat O(n log n); choose among them by speed, space, and stability — not Big-O class.
Quicksort: fastest in practice, in-place, O(n²) worst; mergesort: stable + guaranteed but O(n) space; heapsort: in-place, not stable.
Counting/radix beat the bound (O(n+k)) for bounded integer keys; libraries use hybrids (introsort/Timsort) and switch to insertion sort for small inputs.

cards.title: Stacks & Queues
takeaways: Stack = LIFO (push/pop the top); queue = FIFO (enqueue back, dequeue front) — both O(1) at their ends.
Stacks model nesting/reversal (call stack, undo, parsing, DFS); queues model in-order/fair processing (scheduling, buffers, BFS).
Variants: deque (both ends) and priority queue/heap (dequeue by priority, not arrival).

cards.title: Trees & Graphs
takeaways: Tree = hierarchy, no cycles (BST/heap/trie for sorted/priority/prefix); graph = network with arbitrary edges (adjacency list for sparse).
DFS (stack/recursion) goes deep — paths, cycles, topo-sort; BFS (queue) goes wide — shortest path in unweighted graphs.
Always track visited nodes on graphs (cycles!) — that's the key difference from tree traversal.

cards.title: Branching Strategies
takeaways: Spectrum: Git Flow (heavy, versioned releases) → GitHub Flow (short branches, always-deployable main) → trunk-based (commit to main daily + feature flags).
Core trade-off: branch lifetime vs merge pain — long branches diverge and cause merge hell.
High-performing CI/CD teams favor trunk-based development with small batches and feature flags.

cards.title: How Git Works: Objects & Refs
takeaways: Git stores snapshots, not diffs: blobs (files), trees (dirs), commits (tree + parents), all content-addressed by hash.
Commits form a DAG; a branch is just a movable pointer to a commit (so branching is instant).
Merge = a commit with two parents; rebase = replay commits onto a new base (new hashes).

cards.title: Merge vs Rebase
takeaways: Merge = non-destructive, adds a merge commit (true history); rebase = replays commits for a clean linear history (new hashes).
Common workflow: rebase local branches to stay current, merge (or squash) into main.
Golden rule: never rebase commits others have already pulled — it rewrites shared history.

cards.title: Resolving Merge Conflicts
takeaways: A conflict means two branches changed the same lines; Git pauses and asks you to choose — it never guesses.
Resolve by editing between the markers (<<</===/>>>), then git add and commit/continue; a 3-way merge tool helps.
Prevent conflicts by integrating early, keeping changes small, and standardizing formatting.

cards.title: Undoing Changes: reset, revert, reflog
takeaways: revert = new inverse commit, safe on shared branches; reset = move the branch pointer, for local history only.
reset modes: --soft (keep staged), --mixed (keep in working dir), --hard (discard).
reflog is the safety net — it remembers where HEAD has been (~90 days), so 'lost' commits are usually recoverable.

cards.title: Cache Stampede & Thundering Herd
takeaways: A stampede = a hot key expires and every concurrent request misses and hits the DB at once.
Fix with request coalescing / single-flight (or a rebuild lock): exactly one request recomputes, the rest wait or serve stale.
Stale-while-revalidate removes the synchronous miss; TTL jitter stops many keys expiring in lockstep.

cards.title: Keeping Data Consistent Across Services
takeaways: Avoid distributed transactions (2PC) across services — use sagas: local transactions coordinated by events, with compensations to undo on failure.
Use the transactional outbox to write state + event atomically, so the dual-write problem can't lose events.
Embrace eventual consistency: consumers are idempotent and the UX models 'processing' states between steps.

cards.title: Debugging a Production Latency Spike
takeaways: Start with “what changed?” — a deploy/flag/traffic change is the usual cause; a fast rollback is the fastest mitigation.
Read the golden signals (latency, traffic, errors, saturation) to split overload from a degraded dependency.
Drill metrics → traces → logs to localize tier → span → cause; mitigate first (rollback/scale/timeout), then fix durably.

cards.title: Debugging a Memory Leak in Production
takeaways: Confirm a steady, non-recovering growth trend (not just high load), then capture and diff heap dumps to find the growing type.
A managed leak = objects still referenced (can't be GC'd); find the root — static collections, unremoved event handlers, undisposed resources, unbounded caches.
Mitigate with restarts, but fix at the root: unsubscribe, dispose, and bound caches — GC tuning won't reclaim a rooted object.

cards.title: Designing a Rate Limiter
takeaways: Know the algorithms: fixed/sliding window (simple) vs token/leaky bucket (bursts vs smooth) — token bucket is the common default.
Across a fleet the counter must be a shared, atomic store (Redis INCR/Lua), or each instance allows N× the limit.
Key per user/API-key/IP, enforce at the gateway, and respond with 429 + Retry-After so clients back off.

cards.title: Designing an API for 1 Million Requests/Second
takeaways: A million RPS = a stateless fleet behind a load balancer, not one big server — capacity becomes a dial.
Cache at every layer (edge/CDN + Redis); a high hit rate is what makes the origin survivable.
Keep the request path cheap (async I/O, pooled connections, small payloads) and queue writes; rate-limit at the gateway.

cards.title: Designing Authentication & Sessions
takeaways: Core trade-off: sessions (stateful, easy revoke, needs a store) vs JWTs (stateless, scalable, hard to revoke).
Square the circle with short-lived access tokens + refresh tokens; store access in memory, refresh in an HttpOnly cookie.
Delegate to OAuth2/OIDC to avoid storing passwords; add MFA, throttling, and secure reset flows.

cards.title: Designing So You Never Lose Data
takeaways: Durability is about honest acknowledgments — never confirm a write until it's persisted (WAL + fsync) and replicated.
Synchronous/quorum replication (W + R > N) protects acknowledged data from single-node loss, at a latency cost.
Backups + point-in-time recovery cover what replication can't (bad deploys, accidental deletes); test the restore.

cards.title: Designing for High Availability
takeaways: High availability = no single point of failure: redundancy at every tier, spread across availability zones.
Recovery must be automatic — health checks pull bad instances, replicas get promoted; size it by RTO (recovery time) and RPO (data loss).
Availability multiplies across tiers, and during a partition you trade availability vs consistency (CAP) — so redundancy has to be everywhere.

cards.title: Handling Many Concurrent Users
takeaways: Separate concurrency from throughput; most concurrent users are idle, so the limit is in-flight requests, not total users.
Async, non-blocking I/O is the biggest lever — it frees the thread during waits; never block (.Result/.Wait()) on the request path.
Bound scarce resources (connection pools) and add backpressure / load shedding so the service degrades gracefully instead of collapsing.

cards.title: The Hot Key (Celebrity) Problem
takeaways: Sharding spreads keys, not load — one popular key concentrates on a single shard (hot partition), and more shards don't help.
Hot reads: replicate + cache aggressively (it's the ideal cache target); hot writes: split the key into N sub-counters and sum.
For social fan-out, treat celebrities specially — fan-out-on-read instead of pushing to millions of inboxes.

cards.title: Idempotency in Distributed Systems
takeaways: Timeouts make success unknowable, so clients must retry — and the server must make repeats harmless.
An idempotency key + a persisted (key → result) dedup record turns retries into a replay of the stored result, not a second effect.
Prefer naturally idempotent operations (set a value, not apply a delta); queue consumers are at-least-once, so they must be idempotent too.

cards.title: Managing Secrets & Credentials
takeaways: Never in code or git — a committed secret is a public secret; bots find them in minutes.
Store in a secrets manager/vault, inject at runtime via a managed identity (no bootstrap secret to leak).
Prefer short-lived, least-privilege credentials, rotate regularly, and run secret-scanning in CI.

cards.title: Mitigating a DDoS Attack
takeaways: Classify the attack: volumetric (bandwidth), protocol (connection state), L7 (expensive valid-looking requests).
Absorb at the edge (CDN/scrubbing via anycast) — you can't soak a volumetric flood at a single origin.
Layer WAF + rate limits + challenges + caching + autoscale, and have it configured before the attack.

cards.title: Preventing Cascading Failures
takeaways: Cascades spread through resource exhaustion: callers block on a slow dependency until they run out of threads.
Timeouts (fail fast) + circuit breakers (stop calling the sick service) are the core defenses.
Bulkheads isolate per-dependency resources; retries need backoff + jitter, and a fallback degrades gracefully.

cards.title: Defending Against XSS & CSRF
takeaways: XSS = attacker's script runs on your page → output-encode, CSP, HttpOnly cookies, sanitize.
CSRF = forged authenticated request via auto-sent cookies → SameSite cookies, anti-forgery tokens, Origin checks.
They're distinct threats needing distinct fixes — and HttpOnly cookies (anti-XSS) still require CSRF defense.

cards.title: Read-Heavy vs Write-Heavy Systems
takeaways: Reads and writes scale with opposite tools — ask the read/write ratio first.
Read-heavy → cache + read replicas + denormalize (accept replication lag); write-heavy → shard + batch + queue + LSM stores.
Replicas help reads but tax writes; when a system is both, split the models with CQRS.

cards.title: Reducing Latency for a Global Audience
takeaways: Latency is round trips and distance — fix it by moving data/compute closer (CDN, edge, regional replicas), not by adding CPU.
Static and cacheable content belongs on a CDN; geo-route users to the nearest region (the stateless app tier makes this easy).
Writes are the hard part of going global — single write region (simpler) vs multi-primary (faster, but eventual consistency + conflicts).

cards.title: Responding to a Production Outage
takeaways: Declare an incident and assign an incident commander — don't debug a major outage silently or alone.
Mitigate before root-causing: rollback, failover, scale, or feature-flag off; restoring service beats understanding it.
Diagnose via what-changed + metrics→traces→logs, then run a blameless post-mortem with concrete action items.

cards.title: Ransomware Hit Your Cloud Storage: The First 15 Minutes
takeaways: It's an incident-response question: contain → assess → communicate → recover, and never start fixing a breach solo.
Contain first (revoke keys, lock the bucket); the entry point is almost certainly a compromised write credential.
Recovery depends on immutable, isolated backups (versioning + Object Lock) — set those up before the incident; don't pay the ransom.

cards.title: Scaling a Web Application
takeaways: Scale incrementally, each step forced by a real bottleneck: split tiers → stateless app fleet + LB → cache + CDN → replicas/sharding → async queue.
Horizontal scaling of the app tier requires statelessness — sessions, files, and caches live in shared stores.
The database is the hardest tier; caching and read replicas defer the pain, sharding eventually divides it.

cards.title: Scaling the Database Tier
takeaways: Tune first (indexes, N+1, connection pool) — it often beats adding hardware.
Climb in order: cache → read replicas → partition → shard; each rung targets a different bottleneck and adds complexity.
Sharding is the powerful-but-painful last resort — cross-shard joins/transactions get hard, and the shard key must be high-cardinality and evenly distributed.

cards.title: Securing a REST API
takeaways: Security is layers: TLS → authentication → authorization → input validation → rate limiting → secrets/audit.
The most common failure is broken object-level authorization (BOLA/IDOR) — check ownership on every resource, not just 'is logged in.'
Use proven auth (OAuth2/OIDC), never roll your own crypto, lock down CORS, and follow the OWASP API Top 10.

cards.title: Storing Passwords Securely
takeaways: Never plaintext, never reversible encryption, never a fast hash (MD5/SHA) — use Argon2/bcrypt/scrypt.
A unique salt per user defeats rainbow tables; a tunable work factor makes brute force expensive (and raise it over time).
Use the platform's library, constant-time compare, optionally a pepper, and add MFA — don't roll your own.

cards.title: Surviving Traffic Spikes
takeaways: You can't autoscale in seconds — combine caching (absorb), a queue (flatten), and pre-warming/rate limits (cap).
Turn synchronous spikes into asynchronous streams: accept fast (202), process at a sustainable rate.
Degrade gracefully — stale data, disabled extras, a waiting room — because partial service beats a full outage.

cards.title: Where to Store a JWT on the Frontend
takeaways: It's an XSS vs CSRF trade-off: localStorage is XSS-readable; cookies are auto-sent (CSRF).
Default to HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookies (XSS can't read the token) plus a CSRF token — avoid localStorage for sensitive tokens.
Best practice: short-lived access token in memory + refresh token in an HttpOnly cookie; and remember XSS prevention (CSP) is the real fix.

cards.title: Zero-Downtime Database Migration
takeaways: During a rolling deploy old + new code share the DB, so every step must be backward-compatible — no big-bang destructive change.
Use expand → migrate → contract: add additively, dual-write + backfill in batches, then switch reads and drop the old shape later.
Gate the cutover behind a feature flag and verify with shadow reads; keep each step reversible until the final drop.

cards.title: API Gateway
takeaways: An API gateway is the single entry point that routes to services and centralizes auth, rate limiting, TLS, aggregation, and protocol translation.
The BFF pattern gives each client type (web/mobile) its own tailored gateway.
Keep it thin (no business logic) and highly available — it’s a potential bottleneck and single point of failure.

cards.title: API Versioning
takeaways: An API is a contract — prefer additive, backward-compatible changes (no version needed); version only on breaking changes.
Strategies: URL path (most common), query string, custom header, or media type — pick one, stay consistent.
Run old + new in parallel with a clear deprecation policy; internally, additive changes + contract tests often suffice.

cards.title: Centralized Logging
takeaways: Ship logs from every service to one searchable store (ELK/Loki/Seq) — per-instance container logs are ephemeral and useless alone.
Use structured logging so you can query by field, and stamp a correlation/trace ID to follow one request across services.
Logs + metrics + traces are the three pillars of observability; the shared trace ID links logs to traces.

cards.title: Circuit Breaker
takeaways: A circuit breaker stops calls to a failing dependency, failing fast to keep your service responsive and let the dependency recover.
Three states: Closed (pass + count), Open (reject/fallback), Half-Open (one trial, then close or re-open).
It prevents cascading failures; combine it with timeouts, retries, and a sensible fallback.

cards.title: Distributed Tracing
takeaways: A trace follows one request across services; it’s a tree of spans (each span = one timed operation).
Context propagation via the trace ID (W3C traceparent) ties all the hops to one trace.
OpenTelemetry is the standard; a backend (Jaeger/Zipkin) renders the waterfall to pinpoint the slow service. Tracing + logs + metrics = observability.

cards.title: Docker & Containers
takeaways: A container packages an app + its dependencies into a portable, isolated unit — same behavior everywhere.
Image = immutable layered template; container = a running instance. A Dockerfile is the build recipe; layers are cached.
Containers share the host kernel (lightweight, fast) — unlike VMs, which each ship a full guest OS.

cards.title: Microservices with .NET
takeaways: YARP/Ocelot (gateway), MassTransit (messaging/saga/outbox), Dapr (portable infra building blocks via a sidecar), Aspire (compose + dev-time observability).
gRPC + Polly + OpenTelemetry + Health Checks cover sync calls, resilience, and observability; Docker + K8s for packaging/running.
Each tool implements a pattern from this category — choose per concern, not all at once.

cards.title: Event-Driven Architecture
takeaways: Services publish immutable events and subscribe to the ones they care about — producers don’t know consumers (loose coupling).
Extensible by design: add a new subscriber without changing the producer.
Choreography (decentralized reactions) vs orchestration (a coordinator); the system is eventually consistent; event sourcing stores events as the source of truth.

cards.title: Inter-Service Communication
takeaways: Synchronous (REST/gRPC) is simple and immediate but tightly couples services and risks cascading failures.
Asynchronous (events/queues) decouples services and adds resilience, at the cost of eventual consistency.
Sync for queries; async for commands/events. Avoid chatty deep sync chains; prefer gRPC for internal sync calls.

cards.title: Kubernetes Concepts
takeaways: Kubernetes orchestrates containers declaratively: you describe desired state, it reconciles reality to match.
Core objects: Pod (smallest unit), Deployment (replicas + rolling updates), Service (stable endpoint + LB), Ingress (HTTP routing).
You get self-healing, autoscaling, and zero-downtime rolling updates out of the box.

cards.title: Monolith vs Microservices
takeaways: Monolith = simple, one deploy, easy transactions; microservices = independent deploy/scale + team autonomy.
Microservices trade code complexity for distributed-systems complexity (network, consistency, observability, ops).
Start with a (modular) monolith; extract services only when a real driver — scale, teams, or a clear bounded context — demands it.

cards.title: Outbox Pattern
takeaways: The dual-write problem: a DB write + a broker publish aren’t atomic, so events get lost or sent for nothing.
The outbox writes the event into an outbox table in the same transaction as the data — atomic by construction.
A relay publishes the outbox separately with retries → at-least-once delivery (consumers must be idempotent). CDC/Debezium is the log-tailing variant.

cards.title: Retry, Timeout & Bulkhead
takeaways: Retry transient faults with exponential backoff + jitter, only for idempotent operations, with a cap.
Put a timeout on every remote call — unbounded waits cause cascading failures.
Bulkhead isolates resource pools per dependency so one slow service can’t exhaust everything.

cards.title: Saga Pattern
takeaways: A saga replaces a cross-service ACID transaction with a sequence of local transactions.
On failure, run compensating transactions to semantically undo completed steps (eventual consistency, not rollback).
Choreography (events, decentralized) vs orchestration (a central coordinator/state machine).

cards.title: Service Discovery
takeaways: Instances have dynamic IPs, so services find each other by name via a registry, not hardcoded addresses.
Client-side discovery (caller queries the registry) vs server-side (a stable LB/gateway does it).
Health checks register/deregister instances; Kubernetes provides DNS-based discovery out of the box.

cards.title: Service Mesh
takeaways: A service mesh moves cross-cutting networking (retries, timeouts, mTLS, tracing, traffic shifting) out of app code into sidecar proxies.
Data plane (proxies) does the work; control plane configures them centrally — change policy without redeploying services.
Wins: uniform mTLS, consistent resilience/observability; costs: operational complexity and per-hop latency — worth it at scale.

cards.title: Strangler Fig Pattern
takeaways: Replace a legacy system incrementally — one slice at a time behind a façade — instead of a risky big-bang rewrite.
A routing layer/gateway sends migrated functionality to new services and the rest to the legacy, shifting over time.
It's incremental, reversible, and ships value throughout; the hard parts are shared data during transition and finishing the last slices.

cards.title: Alerting & Dashboards
takeaways: Alert on symptoms (user impact, SLO burn), not causes (CPU) — symptom alerts catch what matters and novel failures.
Every page must be actionable and urgent; alert fatigue from noisy alerts is the real danger — tune ruthlessly.
Dashboards show health at a glance via the golden signals, with drill-downs for investigation.

cards.title: Metrics, SLIs & SLOs
takeaways: Measure the four golden signals (latency, traffic, errors, saturation); pick SLIs that reflect user experience.
SLI = the measure, SLO = the target, SLA = the customer contract (looser than the SLO).
The error budget (gap to 100%) turns reliability-vs-velocity into a number you spend; don't chase 100%.

cards.title: Structured Logging
takeaways: Log events as key-value fields (usually JSON), not concatenated strings — so logs are queryable, not just readable.
Use message templates ({OrderId}) to get a readable message and structured properties, plus a correlation/trace id.
Use appropriate levels; never log secrets/PII, and avoid drowning the signal.

cards.title: The Three Pillars: Logs, Metrics, Traces
takeaways: Observability = understand internal state from outputs (debug unknowns), broader than monitoring (watch knowns).
Three pillars: metrics (is it wrong?), traces (where?), logs (what exactly?).
Use them together — drill metrics → traces → logs, correlated by a shared trace id.

cards.title: Distributed Tracing & Spans
takeaways: A trace is one request's journey as a tree of timed spans; the root is the entry request, children are downstream calls.
Context propagation (a trace id passed in headers, e.g. W3C traceparent) reassembles work across services into one trace.
It's the only signal that localizes latency/failure in a distributed call; standardize on OpenTelemetry, use sampling for volume.

cards.title: Abstract Class vs Interface
takeaways: Interface = a contract/capability with no instance state, implementable by many unrelated types.
Abstract class = a partial base with shared code, state, and constructors — single inheritance only.
Default rule: prefer an interface unless you need shared implementation/state; default interface methods narrow but don’t erase the gap.

cards.title: Abstraction
takeaways: Abstraction exposes the essential operations and hides the implementation behind a contract.
Interfaces/abstract classes let callers depend on the “what,” so implementations are swappable and testable.
Only abstract where there’s real variation — an interface with one forever-implementation is noise.

cards.title: Composition vs Inheritance
takeaways: Inheritance = is-a, fixed and tightly coupled; composition = has-a, assembled and swappable.
Composition avoids the fragile base class problem and lets you change behavior at runtime.
“Favor composition” isn’t “never inherit” — use inheritance for true, stable is-a specialization.

cards.title: Coupling & Cohesion
takeaways: Coupling = inter-module dependence (want it low); cohesion = intra-module focus (want it high).
“Loose coupling, high cohesion” — focused classes wired by stable contracts are easy to change and test.
SRP attacks cohesion; Dependency Inversion attacks coupling — SOLID is largely about these two forces.

cards.title: DRY, KISS & YAGNI
takeaways: DRY = one home for each piece of knowledge; KISS = simplest solution that works; YAGNI = don’t build for an imagined future.
They’re heuristics in tension — over-applying one hurts the others.
DRY targets duplicated knowledge, not look-alike code: the wrong abstraction is costlier than a little duplication.

cards.title: Encapsulation
takeaways: Keep state private and expose a controlled surface so the object enforces its own invariants.
Properties + access modifiers funnel every change through one trustworthy place.
Encapsulation hides data; abstraction hides complexity — related but distinct.

cards.title: Inheritance
takeaways: Inheritance models is-a: a derived class reuses and specializes a base via virtual/override.
C# allows one base class but many interfaces; sealed stops the chain, abstract forces implementation.
Favor composition when you’re only reusing code — deep hierarchies are rigid and fragile.

cards.title: Law of Demeter
takeaways: Talk only to immediate friends (self, parameters, things you create, your fields) — not their friends.
Train-wreck chains (a.GetB().GetC().Do()) couple you to a whole object graph; “tell, don’t ask” instead.
Fluent/LINQ chains returning the same type aren’t violations — it’s about reaching into others’ internals.

cards.title: Polymorphism
takeaways: Polymorphism = one interface, many behaviors; the call resolves to the object’s actual type.
Runtime (virtual/override, dynamic dispatch) vs compile-time (overloading, generics).
It enables Open/Closed — add new types without editing the code that consumes them, killing switch(type) ladders.

cards.title: SOLID Principles
takeaways: SOLID exists for one reason: keep code cheap and safe to change. Every principle attacks rigidity from a different angle.
Dependency Inversion + Open/Closed are the workhorses — program to interfaces and add behavior by adding types, not editing old ones.
They’re heuristics, not commandments. Over-applying SOLID creates its own mess (a factory for every class) — use judgment.

cards.title: Authentication vs Authorization
takeaways: Authentication = who you are (login); authorization = what you're allowed to do (permissions) — authn first, then authz.
Failure codes: 401 (not authenticated) vs 403 (authenticated but forbidden).
Most real breaches are authorization failures — check per-resource ownership, not just 'is logged in.'

cards.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
takeaways: XSS = untrusted input rendered as script, running the attacker's JS in the victim's browser (session theft, actions-as-user).
Three types: stored (worst), reflected, DOM-based.
Defend with output encoding (frameworks auto-encode), a CSP, sanitizing allowed HTML, and HttpOnly cookies.

cards.title: Encryption Basics
takeaways: Symmetric (one shared key, e.g. AES) is fast for bulk data; asymmetric (public/private pair) solves key distribution and identity.
TLS combines them: asymmetric handshake to exchange a symmetric session key.
Encrypt in transit (TLS) and at rest; never roll your own crypto — use vetted libraries.

cards.title: Hashing & Salting
takeaways: Hashing is one-way (no key to reverse it) — store the hash, compare at login; encryption is the wrong tool for passwords.
A per-user salt makes identical passwords hash differently, defeating rainbow tables.
Use a slow, memory-hard password hash (Argon2/bcrypt) with a work factor — never fast MD5/SHA for passwords.

cards.title: SQL Injection
takeaways: SQL injection = untrusted input concatenated into a query, so it executes as code, not data.
The fix is parameterized queries / prepared statements — they bind input strictly as data; ORMs do this by default.
Escaping/blocklists are not reliable; add least-privilege DB accounts and input validation as defense in depth.

cards.title: Caching at Scale
takeaways: Caching is layered: browser → CDN → in-process → distributed (Redis/Memcached) → DB. Serve as high as you can.
The hit ratio is the headline metric; cache read-heavy, expensive-to-compute data.
Memcached = simple fast string cache; Redis = data structures + persistence + extras (usually the pick).

cards.title: CAP Theorem
takeaways: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance — during a partition you can keep at most two.
Partitions are unavoidable, so the real choice is CP (correct, may refuse) vs AP (available, may be stale).
It’s decided per use case (bank = CP, cart/feed = AP); PACELC adds the latency-vs-consistency trade when there’s no partition.

cards.title: CDNs
takeaways: A CDN caches content on edge servers near users — lower latency, big origin offload, spike/DDoS resilience.
Routing is via GeoDNS/anycast to the nearest edge; the origin is hit only on a cache miss.
Control caching with Cache-Control/TTL and ship changes via purge or versioned filenames.

cards.title: Consistent Hashing
takeaways: hash(key) % N remaps almost all keys when N changes — terrible for caches and sharding.
Consistent hashing puts keys and nodes on a ring; a key goes to the next node clockwise, so adding/removing a node moves only ~1/N of keys.
Virtual nodes spread each server across the ring for even load and smoother rebalancing.

cards.title: Horizontal vs Vertical Scaling
takeaways: Vertical = bigger machine (simple, but a ceiling + single point of failure); horizontal = more machines (elastic + fault-tolerant).
Horizontal scaling requires stateless services — move state to a shared store (Redis, DB, object storage).
Start vertical, go horizontal at the ceiling; the database is usually the hardest tier (hence replication/sharding).

cards.title: How Gmail Works
takeaways: Storage is sharded by user (horizontal scale); attachments go to object storage.
Instant search is an inverted index (word → messages), not a mailbox scan; delivery is real-time push over a long-lived connection.
Labels (one message, many tags) beat folders; inbound mail is ML-spam-filtered before it lands.

cards.title: How Instagram's Feed Works
takeaways: A modern feed is a pipeline: candidate generation → ML ranking by predicted engagement → serve.
Media dominates cost — transcode to multiple sizes and deliver from a CDN; metadata is small and sharded.
Time-sortable sharded IDs let billions of posts stay ordered and routable without a central bottleneck.

cards.title: How Netflix Streaming Works
takeaways: ABR encodes each title into many bitrates, chunked into small segments; the player adapts per chunk to current bandwidth.
Chunks are served from CDN edges (Netflix’s Open Connect, inside ISPs), with popular content pre-positioned.
A microservices control plane handles browse/recommendations/auth/DRM — decoupled from serving the video bytes.

cards.title: How Twitter's Timeline Works
takeaways: A home timeline is a fan-out problem: precompute on write (fast reads) or assemble on read (cheap writes).
Pure push breaks for celebrities (millions of list writes per tweet) → use a hybrid: push for normal users, pull for mega-accounts.
Cache timelines in Redis as lists of IDs; hydrate the tweet content separately.

cards.title: Load Balancers & Reverse Proxies
takeaways: A load balancer gives scale + high availability and ejects unhealthy servers via health checks.
L4 (fast, IP/port) vs L7 (HTTP-aware: route by URL/header, TLS termination); algorithms: round-robin, least-connections, IP hash, weighted.
Keep servers stateless (shared session store) so you don’t need sticky sessions.

cards.title: Message Queues
takeaways: Queues decouple producer/consumer, buffer spikes, and add resilience (retries, dead-letter queues).
Traditional queue (RabbitMQ): one consumer, message removed on ack. Log/stream (Kafka): retained, partitioned, replayable, many consumer groups.
Delivery is usually at-least-once → make consumers idempotent.

cards.title: Rate Limiting Algorithms
takeaways: Rate limiting prevents abuse/overload and ensures fairness; over-limit → 429 + Retry-After.
Fixed window (simple, boundary bursts), sliding window (smooth), token bucket (allows bursts — most common), leaky bucket (smooths output).
For multiple servers, use a shared store (Redis) so the limit is global, not per-instance.

cards.title: How Sessions Work
takeaways: HTTP is stateless; a session adds server-side memory keyed by an opaque ID carried in a cookie.
To scale horizontally, move that memory into a shared store (Redis) so any server can resolve any session.
Sessions are stateful on the server — JWTs trade that for client-held, signed state (no server lookup, harder to revoke).

cards.title: Integration Testing
takeaways: Integration tests catch the bugs between units: wiring, SQL, serialization, configuration — what mocks hide.
Use WebApplicationFactory for the real HTTP pipeline and a real DB in a container (Testcontainers), not an in-memory fake.
They're slower and fewer (the pyramid's middle) — keep each isolated with a fresh DB or transaction rollback.

cards.title: Mocking & Test Doubles
takeaways: Test doubles (dummy, stub, fake, spy, mock) stand in for real dependencies to isolate the unit.
Stubs/fakes support state verification; mocks/spies verify interactions — use mocks when the call is the behavior.
Don't over-mock: double real boundaries (I/O, services), use the real thing for pure logic.

cards.title: Test-Driven Development
takeaways: TDD = red-green-refactor: failing test first, simplest code to pass, then clean up — in tiny cycles.
It's a design discipline: writing the test first surfaces coupling and pushes toward testable, modular code.
Best for logic-rich code; even without strict TDD, 'hard to test ⇒ probably badly designed' is the lesson worth keeping.

cards.title: The Test Pyramid
takeaways: Write many fast unit tests, fewer integration tests, very few slow E2E tests.
Inverting it (the ice-cream cone) gives a slow, flaky suite that can't localize failures.
Push tests down: prefer a unit test to an E2E test whenever it catches the same bug.

cards.title: Unit Testing
takeaways: A unit test checks one unit in isolation, fast and deterministic, so failures localize precisely.
Structure as Arrange-Act-Assert; keep tests FIRST (Fast, Isolated, Repeatable, Self-validating, Timely).
Test behavior, not implementation, so tests survive refactors instead of obstructing them.

cards.title: Content Negotiation
takeaways: Clients state preferences with Accept/Accept-Language/Accept-Encoding; the server returns the best representation and names it in Content-*.
Always send Vary so caches keep a separate copy per negotiated variant.
The everyday case is compression (gzip/brotli) — a large, near-free transfer-size win.

cards.title: How Cookies Work
takeaways: The server sets a cookie via Set-Cookie; the browser auto-sends it on every request to the domain.
Always use HttpOnly + Secure for auth cookies, and SameSite to blunt CSRF.
No expiry = session cookie; with expiry = persistent. Keep cookies tiny (store an ID, not data).

cards.title: CORS
takeaways: The same-origin policy blocks cross-origin reads; CORS is the server opting specific origins back in.
Non-simple requests trigger a preflight OPTIONS the server must approve.
CORS is browser-enforced and protects users — it is not server security, and * can’t be combined with credentials.

cards.title: How DNS Works
takeaways: DNS maps names to IPs via a cached hierarchy: resolver → root → TLD → authoritative.
Key record types: A/AAAA (address), CNAME (alias), MX (mail), TXT, NS.
TTL controls caching — low TTL before changes for fast propagation, high TTL for fewer lookups.

cards.title: HTTP Caching
takeaways: Freshness (max-age) serves from cache with no network; validation (ETag/Last-Modified) yields a cheap 304 when unchanged.
no-cache = revalidate before use (not “don’t cache”); no-store = never store.
Cache static assets hard and bust the cache with content-hashed filenames.

cards.title: HTTP Status Codes
takeaways: The first digit = the class: 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error.
Know the confusable pairs: 401 (who are you?) vs 403 (not allowed); 301 (permanent) vs 302 (temporary).
Use 201 + Location for creates, 204 for empty successes, 409 for conflicts, 429 for rate limits.

cards.title: HTTP Fundamentals
takeaways: HTTP is a stateless request/response protocol: method + path + headers + body ↔ status + headers + body.
Honor method semantics: GET is safe/idempotent, PUT/DELETE idempotent, POST is not.
HTTP/2 multiplexes streams over one connection; HTTP/3 runs on QUIC/UDP — same model, faster transport.

cards.title: HTTPS & TLS
takeaways: TLS gives confidentiality, integrity, and server authentication; HTTPS = HTTP over TLS.
The handshake uses slow asymmetric keys to agree a fast symmetric session key, then encrypts traffic with it.
A CA-signed certificate proves the server’s identity — that’s what defeats man-in-the-middle attacks.

cards.title: JWT & Stateless Auth
takeaways: A JWT is header.payload.signature; validating = re-checking the signature and expiry, with no server lookup.
It’s signed, not encrypted — readable by anyone, so no secrets in the payload.
Stateless and scalable, but hard to revoke before expiry → short-lived tokens + refresh flow.

cards.title: OAuth 2.0 & OpenID Connect
takeaways: OAuth 2.0 = delegated authorization: an app gets a scoped access token, never your password.
Use the Authorization Code flow with PKCE; scopes limit access, refresh tokens renew it.
OpenID Connect adds an ID token for identity — OAuth = access, OIDC = login.

cards.title: REST API Design
takeaways: Model resources as nouns with URIs; use HTTP methods as the verbs — no action URLs.
Be stateless and use correct status codes — that’s “level 2” REST and what most great APIs ship.
Uniform, predictable interfaces make an API learnable; HATEOAS is optional and rarely used.

cards.title: WebSockets vs Polling vs SSE
takeaways: Polling repeats requests (simple, wasteful); SSE is a one-way server→client stream; WebSockets are full-duplex over one persistent connection.
Choose by direction: one-way push → SSE; chatty two-way low-latency → WebSockets.
SignalR abstracts the choice with automatic transport fallback.
